title: VII
html:
VII

I got hold of Mrs.¬ÝGrose as soon after this as I could; and I can give no intelligible account of how I fought out the interval. Yet I still hear myself cry as I fairly threw myself into her arms: ‚ÄúThey knowÔªø‚Äîit‚Äôs too monstrous: they know, they know!‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd what on earthÔªø‚Äî?‚Äù I felt her incredulity as she held me.

‚ÄúWhy, all that we knowÔªø‚Äîand heaven knows what else besides!‚Äù Then, as she released me, I made it out to her, made it out perhaps only now with full coherency even to myself. ‚ÄúTwo hours ago, in the garden‚ÄùÔªø‚ÄîI could scarce articulateÔªø‚Äî‚ÄúFlora saw!‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝGrose took it as she might have taken a blow in the stomach. ‚ÄúShe has told you?‚Äù she panted.

‚ÄúNot a wordÔªø‚Äîthat‚Äôs the horror. She kept it to herself! The child of eight, that child!‚Äù Unutterable still, for me, was the stupefaction of it.

Mrs.¬ÝGrose, of course, could only gape the wider. ‚ÄúThen how do you know?‚Äù

‚ÄúI was thereÔªø‚ÄîI saw with my eyes: saw that she was perfectly aware.‚Äù

‚ÄúDo you mean aware of him?‚Äù

‚ÄúNoÔªø‚Äîof her.‚Äù I was conscious as I spoke that I looked prodigious things, for I got the slow reflection of them in my companion‚Äôs face. ‚ÄúAnother personÔªø‚Äîthis time; but a figure of quite as unmistakable horror and evil: a woman in black, pale and dreadfulÔªø‚Äîwith such an air also, and such a face!Ôªø‚Äîon the other side of the lake. I was there with the childÔªø‚Äîquiet for the hour; and in the midst of it she came.‚Äù

‚ÄúCame howÔªø‚Äîfrom where?‚Äù

‚ÄúFrom where they come from! She just appeared and stood thereÔªø‚Äîbut not so near.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd without coming nearer?‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, for the effect and the feeling, she might have been as close as you!‚Äù

My friend, with an odd impulse, fell back a step. ‚ÄúWas she someone you‚Äôve never seen?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes. But someone the child has. Someone you have.‚Äù Then, to show how I had thought it all out: ‚ÄúMy predecessorÔªø‚Äîthe one who died.‚Äù

‚ÄúMiss Jessel?‚Äù

‚ÄúMiss Jessel. You don‚Äôt believe me?‚Äù I pressed.

She turned right and left in her distress. ‚ÄúHow can you be sure?‚Äù

This drew from me, in the state of my nerves, a flash of impatience. ‚ÄúThen ask FloraÔªø‚Äîshe‚Äôs sure!‚Äù But I had no sooner spoken than I caught myself up. ‚ÄúNo, for God‚Äôs sake, don‚Äôt! She‚Äôll say she isn‚ÄôtÔªø‚Äîshe‚Äôll lie!‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝGrose was not too bewildered instinctively to protest. ‚ÄúAh, how can you?‚Äù

‚ÄúBecause I‚Äôm clear. Flora doesn‚Äôt want me to know.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs only then to spare you.‚Äù

‚ÄúNo, noÔªø‚Äîthere are depths, depths! The more I go over it, the more I see in it, and the more I see in it, the more I fear. I don‚Äôt know what I don‚Äôt seeÔªø‚Äîwhat I don‚Äôt fear!‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝGrose tried to keep up with me. ‚ÄúYou mean you‚Äôre afraid of seeing her again?‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, no; that‚Äôs nothingÔªø‚Äînow!‚Äù Then I explained. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs of not seeing her.‚Äù

But my companion only looked wan. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt understand you.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy, it‚Äôs that the child may keep it upÔªø‚Äîand that the child assuredly willÔªø‚Äîwithout my knowing it.‚Äù

At the image of this possibility Mrs.¬ÝGrose for a moment collapsed, yet presently to pull herself together again, as if from the positive force of the sense of what, should we yield an inch, there would really be to give way to. ‚ÄúDear, dearÔªø‚Äîwe must keep our heads! And after all, if she doesn‚Äôt mind itÔªø‚Äî!‚Äù She even tried a grim joke. ‚ÄúPerhaps she likes it!‚Äù

‚ÄúLikes such thingsÔªø‚Äîa scrap of an infant!‚Äù

‚ÄúIsn‚Äôt it just a proof of her blessed innocence?‚Äù my friend bravely inquired.

She brought me, for the instant, almost round. ‚ÄúOh, we must clutch at thatÔªø‚Äîwe must cling to it! If it isn‚Äôt a proof of what you say, it‚Äôs a proof ofÔªø‚ÄîGod knows what! For the woman‚Äôs a horror of horrors.‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝGrose, at this, fixed her eyes a minute on the ground; then at last raising them, ‚ÄúTell me how you know,‚Äù she said.

‚ÄúThen you admit it‚Äôs what she was?‚Äù I cried.

‚ÄúTell me how you know,‚Äù my friend simply repeated.

‚ÄúKnow? By seeing her! By the way she looked.‚Äù

‚ÄúAt you, do you meanÔªø‚Äîso wickedly?‚Äù

‚ÄúDear me, noÔªø‚ÄîI could have borne that. She gave me never a glance. She only fixed the child.‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝGrose tried to see it. ‚ÄúFixed her?‚Äù

‚ÄúAh, with such awful eyes!‚Äù

She stared at mine as if they might really have resembled them. ‚ÄúDo you mean of dislike?‚Äù

‚ÄúGod help us, no. Of something much worse.‚Äù

‚ÄúWorse than dislike?‚ÄùÔªø‚Äîthis left her indeed at a loss.

‚ÄúWith a determinationÔªø‚Äîindescribable. With a kind of fury of intention.‚Äù

I made her turn pale. ‚ÄúIntention?‚Äù

‚ÄúTo get hold of her.‚Äù Mrs.¬ÝGroseÔªø‚Äîher eyes just lingering on mineÔªø‚Äîgave a shudder and walked to the window; and while she stood there looking out I completed my statement. ‚ÄúThat‚Äôs what Flora knows.‚Äù

After a little she turned round. ‚ÄúThe person was in black, you say?‚Äù

‚ÄúIn mourningÔªø‚Äîrather poor, almost shabby. ButÔªø‚ÄîyesÔªø‚Äîwith extraordinary beauty.‚Äù I now recognized to what I had at last, stroke by stroke, brought the victim of my confidence, for she quite visibly weighed this. ‚ÄúOh, handsomeÔªø‚Äîvery, very,‚Äù I insisted; ‚Äúwonderfully handsome. But infamous.‚Äù

She slowly came back to me. ‚ÄúMiss JesselÔªø‚Äîwas infamous.‚Äù She once more took my hand in both her own, holding it as tight as if to fortify me against the increase of alarm I might draw from this disclosure. ‚ÄúThey were both infamous,‚Äù she finally said.

So, for a little, we faced it once more together; and I found absolutely a degree of help in seeing it now so straight. ‚ÄúI appreciate,‚Äù I said, ‚Äúthe great decency of your not having hitherto spoken; but the time has certainly come to give me the whole thing.‚Äù She appeared to assent to this, but still only in silence; seeing which I went on: ‚ÄúI must have it now. Of what did she die? Come, there was something between them.‚Äù

‚ÄúThere was everything.‚Äù

‚ÄúIn spite of the differenceÔªø‚Äî?‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, of their rank, their condition‚ÄùÔªø‚Äîshe brought it woefully out. ‚ÄúShe was a lady.‚Äù

I turned it over; I again saw. ‚ÄúYesÔªø‚Äîshe was a lady.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd he so dreadfully below,‚Äù said Mrs.¬ÝGrose.

I felt that I doubtless needn‚Äôt press too hard, in such company, on the place of a servant in the scale; but there was nothing to prevent an acceptance of my companion‚Äôs own measure of my predecessor‚Äôs abasement. There was a way to deal with that, and I dealt; the more readily for my full visionÔªø‚Äîon the evidenceÔªø‚Äîof our employer‚Äôs late clever, good-looking ‚Äúown‚Äù man; impudent, assured, spoiled, depraved. ‚ÄúThe fellow was a hound.‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝGrose considered as if it were perhaps a little a case for a sense of shades. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve never seen one like him. He did what he wished.‚Äù

‚ÄúWith her?‚Äù

‚ÄúWith them all.‚Äù

It was as if now in my friend‚Äôs own eyes Miss Jessel had again appeared. I seemed at any rate, for an instant, to see their evocation of her as distinctly as I had seen her by the pond; and I brought out with decision: ‚ÄúIt must have been also what she wished!‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝGrose‚Äôs face signified that it had been indeed, but she said at the same time: ‚ÄúPoor womanÔªø‚Äîshe paid for it!‚Äù

‚ÄúThen you do know what she died of?‚Äù I asked.

‚ÄúNoÔªø‚ÄîI know nothing. I wanted not to know; I was glad enough I didn‚Äôt; and I thanked heaven she was well out of this!‚Äù

‚ÄúYet you had, then, your ideaÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúOf her real reason for leaving? Oh, yesÔªø‚Äîas to that. She couldn‚Äôt have stayed. Fancy it hereÔªø‚Äîfor a governess! And afterward I imaginedÔªø‚Äîand I still imagine. And what I imagine is dreadful.‚Äù

‚ÄúNot so dreadful as what I do,‚Äù I replied; on which I must have shown herÔªø‚Äîas I was indeed but too consciousÔªø‚Äîa front of miserable defeat. It brought out again all her compassion for me, and at the renewed touch of her kindness my power to resist broke down. I burst, as I had, the other time, made her burst, into tears; she took me to her motherly breast, and my lamentation overflowed. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt do it!‚Äù I sobbed in despair; ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt save or shield them! It‚Äôs far worse than I dreamedÔªø‚Äîthey‚Äôre lost!‚Äù
